title: Staying Cool and Well Through Summer (Grishma)
excerpt: Ayurveda calls summer Grishma, a season that drains your vital energy and heats up both body and mind. Here is how the classical texts guide you to stay cool, nourished, and well from May through July.
at_a_glance: Summer (Grishma) depletes bala, vital strength, more than any other season, so lighter food and more rest are genuinely protective.
Cooling, sweet, liquid foods and drinks align with classical texts and are also simply the most comfortable things to eat in Indian summers.
Avoid intense midday sun and heavy meals; a short afternoon rest is permitted and recommended in the Grishma regimen.

body.heading: why summer hits you differently
paragraphs: You know the feeling: it is ten in the morning, the sun has barely climbed, and you already feel drained, thirsty, and a little irritable. You sleep eight hours but wake up tired. Your appetite drops even though you skipped dinner. This is not weakness. This is your body responding to Grishma, the summer season in the Ayurvedic calendar, which runs roughly from mid-May to mid-July when the sun is at its most intense.

The classical texts describe Grishma as the season when the sun's sharp, drying rays draw out moisture from the earth and from living beings (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). The body loses what Ayurveda calls bala, vital strength or immunity, more rapidly in summer than at any other time of the year. Pitta dosha, the force of heat and transformation in the body, naturally rises. But interestingly, Kapha, which kept you insulated through winter, begins to melt and weaken. The result is that you enter summer already a little depleted and spend the season fighting a slow internal drain.
body.pullQuote: Summer's heat is countered by coolness; its dryness is countered by moisture; its lightness is countered by gentle nourishment.

body.heading: what the granthas say about grishma ritucharya
paragraphs: Ritucharya means seasonal regimen, a framework the classical texts lay out season by season so that daily habits can stay in step with nature's rhythms. For Grishma, the Ashtanga Hridaya (Sutrasthana, Ritucharya Adhyaya) recommends foods and drinks that are sweet, light, liquid, and cooling. Rice cooked soft, milk, ghee, and fruit with high water content are specifically favoured. Heavy, sour, salty, and pungent foods aggravate the already-rising Pitta and should be reduced.

The Caraka Samhita (Sutrasthana) similarly underlines the importance of cold water, thin gruels made from barley, and the use of sandalwood paste or cool water for the body in this season. These are not arbitrary recommendations. They follow a core Ayurvedic logic: qualities that are opposite to the aggravated season counteract its excess. Summer's heat is countered by coolness; its dryness is countered by moisture; its lightness is countered by gentle nourishment.

Sushruta Samhita (Sutrasthana) adds the important counsel that one should avoid excessive physical exertion and exposure to the midday sun during Grishma. The sun's rays between ten in the morning and three in the afternoon are considered most depleting. Even sexual activity was traditionally recommended to be moderated in this season because it is considered to deplete ojas, the subtle essence of vitality, which is already under pressure from the heat.

body.heading: simple everyday habits that actually help
paragraphs: The gap between classical wisdom and a real summer day in an Indian city is not as wide as it seems. Most of what the texts recommend is ordinary and affordable.

Start with water. Room-temperature or slightly cool water is better than ice-cold water, which can shock the digestive fire. A clay pot (matka) keeps water at exactly the right coolness. Buttermilk churned thin with a pinch of roasted cumin and a small piece of ginger is one of the great summer drinks in Ayurveda, praised across texts including the Bhavaprakasha Nighantu for its digestive and cooling properties. Coconut water fits the same brief. Sugarcane juice with a squeeze of lime is another classical summer drink described in old seasonal regimens.

Eat lighter than usual. A large heavy meal in summer puts extra demand on a digestive system that is already conserving energy. Moong dal, rice kanji (a thin rice gruel), cooling vegetables like bottle gourd and cucumber, and ripe sweet fruits such as mango and melon all align with the Grishma diet. Mango eaten with milk, a combination beloved across India, is actually endorsed by Ayurvedic texts as a nourishing summer food when the mango is fully ripe and sweet, not sour.

Rest in the afternoon. The texts permit a short daytime rest in summer because the nights are shorter and the body genuinely needs recovery time. A rest of twenty to thirty minutes is very different from a long afternoon sleep, which can produce heaviness and disturb the night.

Keep the body cool externally. A bath in the morning with cool water, loose cotton clothing, and avoiding direct sun in the middle of the day are all part of classical Grishma recommendations. Sandalwood paste on the forehead or temples, rose water on the face, and keeping rooms ventilated are extensions of the same principle.

body.heading: when to see a doctor or vaidya
paragraphs: The guidelines above are general seasonal advice for a healthy adult. They are not a substitute for medical care. If you experience severe dehydration, signs of heat stroke such as confusion, a very high body temperature, or stopping sweating in intense heat, these are emergencies requiring immediate medical attention.

Persistent exhaustion that does not improve with rest, repeated headaches through the summer, very dark urine, or a sudden drop in appetite lasting more than a few days are signs you should consult a doctor or a qualified vaidya rather than self-managing. People with diabetes, kidney conditions, heart disease, or those on medication should not make dietary changes based on seasonal texts without first checking with their treating physician. Ayurvedic recommendations work best as supportive habits alongside, not instead of, professional medical guidance.

try_this_today.title: Matka water through the day
try_this_today.description: Fill an unglazed clay pot each morning. Drink from it through the day instead of refrigerated water. Clay-cooled water sits at a temperature that is cooling without shocking digestion.

try_this_today.title: Thin buttermilk after lunch
try_this_today.description: Churn plain yogurt with three parts water until frothy, strain, and add a pinch of roasted cumin powder and a small pinch of rock salt. Drink a small glass after the midday meal. This combination supports digestion and replaces the fluids lost to heat.

try_this_today.title: A short afternoon rest, not a nap
try_this_today.description: Lie down for twenty to thirty minutes after lunch without fully sleeping. The texts distinguish between a short rest, which the body benefits from in summer, and a long deep afternoon sleep, which creates heaviness.

try_this_today.title: Moong dal and rice as a light dinner
try_this_today.description: Switch heavy lentils and wheat rotis at dinner for soft moong dal khichdi. It is easy to digest, adequately nourishing, and does not add heat the way heavier pulses or fried food can.

try_this_today.title: Early-morning outdoor time only
try_this_today.description: If you walk or exercise outdoors, finish before nine in the morning. Midday exertion is singled out in the Grishma regimen as particularly depleting. Evening walks after five are also fine once the direct sun intensity drops.

ingredients.name: Cumin
ingredients.description: Roasted cumin added to buttermilk or water aids digestion and is a classical Grishma ingredient.

ingredients.name: Ginger
ingredients.description: Small amounts of fresh ginger in buttermilk support digestive fire without adding significant heat.

words_to_know.definition: The summer season in Ayurveda's six-season calendar, roughly mid-May to mid-July in India.
Seasonal regimen: a set of daily habits recommended in the classical texts for each of the six seasons.
Vital strength or immunity. The texts note that bala is at its lowest during Grishma.
The subtle essence of vitality, considered the finest product of good digestion and nourishment.
One of the three doshas, governing heat, digestion, and transformation. It rises naturally in summer.

sources: Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ritucharya Adhyaya · Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana · Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana · Bhavaprakasha,
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
